title: Skill learning and consolidation in healthy humans
description: This longitudinal neuroimaging study investigates neural mechanisms of motor skill learning, consolidation, and retention using magnetoencephalography (MEG) combined with structural MRI. Thirty-one healthy right-handed adults performed a sequential finger-tapping task during an initial training session followed by retest sessions at approximately 24 hours and 30 days post-training. The dataset includes continuous MEG recordings, high-resolution anatomical MRI, behavioral performance metrics, eye-tracking, pupillometry, and electromyography data synchronized across all sessions.
methods_description: Anatomical MRI was acquired using 3T scanners (GE Excite HDxt and Siemens Skyra) with T1-weighted MPRAGE sequences (1mm³ isotropic). Continuous MEG and EMG data were recorded on a CTF-275 system at 600 Hz sampling frequency in a magnetically shielded room. Head position was localized using three coils mapped to individual MRI volumes via BrainSight. Eye-tracking and pupillometry were acquired using an EyeLink 1000 Plus system. Behavioral responses were collected using E-Prime 2 and a Cedrus response pad. Participants performed a sequential finger-tapping task (5-item numerical sequence) with their non-dominant left hand across three MEG sessions: initial training, 24-hour retest, and 30-day retest.
